markdown:
## Graphical Modelling


1. Graphical models are essential tools for **communicating design ideas**. 
2. They help designers **explore** **possibilities**, **refine** **concepts**, and **present solutions** effectively.

<definition term="Graphical Models">**Graphical Models**: **Visual representations** used to **communicate** design **ideas**, **explore solutions**, and **refine** **concepts**.</definition>

The development of ideas through graphical models allows designers to explore and deepen their understanding of a problem and its context.

![I have no idea what this is, but this is a graphic model :)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/2a5a22eb61b8b0f41f8a33cdedf81eb8c1dd14df-781x503.png)

###


## Sketching vs. Formal Drawing Techniques


1. **Graphical models** are **2D representations** used by designers to explore and communicate ideas. 
2. Different techniques help at different stages of the design process.



#### Sketching (Quick & Easy)


1. **Fast and rough** – Used at the start of the design process.
2. **Explores many ideas quickly** – Helps generate and refine concepts.
3. **No strict rules** – Freehand drawings that focus on creativity.
4. **Communicates early thoughts** – Helps share ideas with clients and the design team.

<callout type="example">**Example:** A car designer quickly **sketches multiple** car shapes before **choosing one** to **refine**</callout>



#### Formal Drawing Techniques (Precise & Technical)


As the design develops, more detailed and accurate drawings are needed:

1. **Isometric Drawings** – 3D-like drawings used to show depth and dimensions.
2. **Orthographic Drawings** – Multi-view (top, front, side) diagrams that show exact measurements.
3. **CAD Renderings** – Digital 3D models that provide a high-fidelity version of the design.



## How designers communicate a 3D view of an object


1. Orthographic drawings
2. Isometric drawings
3. Part and assembly drawings

### Orthographic Drawings


1. **Orthographic drawings** are technical 2D drawings that show different sides of an object **without perspective**. 
2. Instead of looking like a 3D sketch, they present **flat views** of each side (top, front, side) to give an **accurate representation** of the object's **shape** and **dimensions**.
3. **Features**
  1. **Show the "side" of a product** – They display different views separately, such as the **front, top, and side views**.
  2. **No vanishing point or perspective** – Unlike 3D sketches, everything is drawn to scale without distortion.
  3. **Accurate representation of form** – They include exact **dimensions** and proportions, making them useful for manufacturing.

<callout type="note">#### Applications of Orthographic Drawings


1. **Used in planning drawings** – Helps designers communicate precise **size, shape, and form** before production.
2. **Essential for manufacturing** – Factories and engineers use these drawings to build parts accurately.
3. **Standard in technical fields** – Common in architecture, product design, and engineering.</callout>

<callout type="example">#### Example


1. Imagine designing a **smartphone case**
2. An **orthographic drawing** would show:
  1. **Front view** (flat image of the front)
  2. **Side view** (thickness and shape from the side)
  3. **Top view** (how wide and deep it is)
3. This ensures manufacturers **know the exact dimensions** to produce an accurate final product.</callout>

![Not to be confused with Orthodontists](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/eac2f25714befd9438598a5d5ca258fbf35243a9-480x360.png)



### Isometric Drawings


1. **Isometric drawings** are 3D-like drawings that show the shape and form of an object clearly. 
2. **Unlike perspective** drawings, **they do not have vanishing points**, meaning all sides remain at a consistent scale.
3. **Features**
  1. **No vanishing points** – Objects don’t shrink or distort as they move further away.
  2. **Drawn on a 30°/90°/30° grid** – The horizontal edges are tilted at **30 degrees**, while vertical edges stay at **90 degrees**.
  3. **Represents 3D objects accurately** – It allows designers to show multiple sides of an object in a single drawing.

<callout type="note">#### Applications of Isometric Drawings


1. **Used to communicate the overall form of a product** – Gives a clear idea of how a design looks from different angles.
2. **Helpful in product and industrial design** – Used by engineers, designers, and architects to visualize objects.
3. **Common in instruction manuals and technical drawings** – Many assembly guides (e.g., LEGO or IKEA) use isometric drawings to show how parts fit together.</callout>

<callout type="example">#### **Example**


1. Imagine designing a **chair**
2. An **isometric drawing** would show:
  1. **Front, top, and side views in one image** (without distortion)
  2. **Accurate proportions and form**
  3. **Easy-to-read design for manufacturers**
3. This makes it a great way to **communicate ideas clearly** before moving to CAD modeling or physical prototypes.</callout>

![You can immediately tell what the product may look like in real life](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/7789fdb9e963aedcc58afe2effca4d250b4718e7-750x375.png)



### Part and Assembly Drawings


1. When designing complex objects with multiple components, **part and assembly drawings** help show how the pieces fit together. 
2. They are essential for manufacturers, engineers, and designers to **understand the structure and assembly process**.

#### Exploded Isometric Drawings


1. An **exploded isometric drawing** is a special type of isometric drawing that shows how different parts of an object come together.
2. The parts are drawn **separately but in the correct order of assembly**, making it clear how they connect.
3. **Features of Exploded Isometric Drawings**
  1. **Shows how an object is assembled** – Displays parts in an exploded view to illustrate their relationships.
  2. **Maintains isometric projection** – No vanishing points, just like regular isometric drawings.
  3. **Clearly communicates assembly sequence** – Helps users understand the correct order of putting parts together.

<callout type="example">A **bicycle** exploded isometric drawing might show the **frame, wheels, chain**, and seat as separate **pieces floating apart**, illustrating **how** **they fit together**.</callout>



![Exploded Isometric Drawings](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/8232e3fbc0fc832969026c439395bf1115a24c12-736x614.png)

#### Assembly Drawings


1. **Assembly drawings** show how multiple components come together to form a finished product. 
2. They can be presented as **exploded drawings** or more **compact, final views**.

#### Features of Assembly Drawings


1. **Illustrates how a product is assembled** – Can be exploded or show the fully built object.
2. **Includes names and numbers of parts** – Helps identify each component.
3. **Contains a Bill of Materials (BOM)** – A list that details **all parts, materials, and quantities** needed for production.

![I love Ikea manuals. Zero words ... just assembly diagrams](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/60b840f60f5e9d85aa28fd4c04960c5323b59d01-800x589.png)

<callout type="example">A **car engine assembly drawing** will label each part (pistons, spark plugs, cylinders) and include a **BOM listing materials, screws, and tools** required.</callout>



### Perspective Drawings - Simple Explanation


1. A **perspective drawing** shows an object **as it would appear in real life** from a specific viewpoint.
2. It creates a sense of **depth and space** by using **foreshortening** and **vanishing points** to make objects look more realistic.

#### Features of Perspective Drawings


1. **Shows objects from a single viewpoint** – Mimics how the human eye sees things.
2. **Uses foreshortening** – Objects appear smaller as they get farther away.
3. **Includes vanishing points** – Lines converge at one or more points on the horizon to create depth.

<callout type="example">If you draw a long road, it will look like the two sides get closer together as they go into the distance.</callout>

#### Applications of Perspective Drawings


1. **Used in early design stages** – Helps visualize how a design might look in a real setting.
2. **Common in architecture and product design** – Makes sketches more relatable for clients.
3. **Familiar to most people** – This is how we naturally see the world, making it easier to understand.

<callout type="note">Architects use **perspective drawings** to show how a house or building will look from a person’s point of view.</callout>

![Perspective diagrams](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/1d680e3d6eb8cf0211c8eba71c733c26a6326690-567x407.png)



### Comparison of Different Graphical Models


| Model | Advantages | Disadvantages |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Sketching | Quick, no tools needed | Lacks detail, may not represent the final product |
| Orthographic | Highly accurate, clear for manufacturing | Requires CAD skills, time-consuming |
| Isometric | Shows overall view, easily understood | Lacks detail, may not resemble the finished product |
| Perspective | Realistic, easily understood | Time-consuming, lacks accuracy, requires skill |
| Assembly | Clear for clients and manufacturers | Time-consuming, requires CAD skills |



## Reflection


<callout type="self_review">1. What are the key differences between orthographic and isometric drawings? 
2. How do annotations enhance the effectiveness of graphical models?
3. Why is it important to choose the right type of graphical model for a specific context?</callout>
